questions.question: Why do nearby-search systems usually separate candidate retrieval from exact distance ranking?
options: Because a fast approximate geo index narrows the set before more exact checks run.
Because exact distance math cannot be computed in distributed systems.
Because ranking and filtering are identical once data is partitioned.
Because nearby search never needs business filters after lookup.
questions.explanation: Approximate lookup keeps the search set bounded, then exact checks preserve correctness.

questions.question: What makes nearby search harder than a static place lookup?
options: Moving objects continuously update the index, so freshness and write amplification become central.
Static places always require full-table scans at query time.
Distance cannot be estimated until after payment is processed.
Geo partitioning only matters for map-rendering systems.
questions.explanation: The moving write path is the real pressure point in proximity systems.

questions.question: What is the main reason dense urban cells become dangerous in proximity systems?
options: They create extreme read and write skew that can overload one shard or candidate set.
They make exact geometry mathematically impossible.
They force all data into one global database transaction.
They remove the need for caching because locality is perfect.
questions.explanation: Hotspot skew, not geometry, is the usual operational problem.

questions.question: Which signal best reveals stale-location risk on the serving path?
options: The age distribution of the latest accepted object updates relative to the freshness target.
The number of product managers reading the dashboard.
The ratio of successful reads to total API routes in the company.
The total count of configured geo cells in the control plane.
questions.explanation: Freshness age directly measures how old returned locations can be.

questions.question: Why is separating availability from the geo index often useful?
options: It reduces write amplification when business state changes more often than coordinates.
It guarantees exact global ordering across all updates.
It removes the need for a fallback radius during low supply.
It ensures every query can be served from a browser cache.
questions.explanation: Different data changes at different rates, so separating them protects the write path.

questions.question: What is the most credible fallback when a query finds too few nearby candidates?
options: Widen the search radius in controlled steps with caps and clear ranking logic.
Randomly choose objects from distant regions to fill the list.
Disable exact distance checks until traffic normalizes.
Repartition the whole geo index synchronously for that request.
questions.explanation: Progressive widening is a product and systems decision, not a random fill.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with replicating hot cells to more shards?
options: It lowers hotspot pressure but increases control-plane and consistency complexity.
It eliminates the need for exact reranking entirely.
It makes stale updates impossible by definition.
It guarantees lower write cost than a single-shard design.
questions.explanation: Replication buys read resilience but creates more coordination and update work.

questions.question: Which redesign is most likely if availability changes every second but coordinates change rarely?
options: Push more filtering into a fast availability layer while keeping geo membership relatively stable.
Recompute every geo partition from scratch on each query.
Store all objects in one relational row to simplify joins.
Replace all TTLs with manual operator updates.
questions.explanation: When business state changes fastest, that layer deserves the lighter update path.
